Applying the [general procedure for interpreting a regression coefficient](Linear-models-overview.qmd#def-coef-interp-procedure) to the log-rate linear predictor $\eta(\vx) = \logf{\lambda(\vx)}$ shows that $\b_j$ is the difference in log-rates per one-unit increase in $x_j$, holding all other covariates fixed (and equal to their reference values when interactions are present).

::: notes
Differences on the log-rate scale become ratios on the rate scale,
because
:::

$$\exp{a-b} = \frac{\exp{a}}{\exp{b}}$$

(recall from [Algebra 2](math-prereqs.qmd#cor-exp-sum))

Therefore, according to this model,
**differences of $\delta$ in covariate $x_j$ correspond to rate ratios of $\exp{\b_j \cdot \delta}$**.

That is, letting $\vX_{-j}$ denote vector $\vX$ with element $j$ removed:

$$
\ba
&\phantom{={}} \logf{\E{Y |\red{X_j = a}, \vX_{-j}=\vx_{-j},T=t}} \\
&\phantom{={}} -\logf{\E{Y |\red{X_j = b}, \vX_{-j}=\vx_{-j},T=t}} \\
&= \logf{t} + \b_0 + \b_1 x_1 + \lds + \red{\b_j (a)} + \lds + \b_p x_p \\
&\phantom{={}} -\logf{t} - \b_0 - \b_1 x_1 - \lds - \red{\b_j (b)} - \lds - \b_p x_p \\
&= \red{\b_j(a-b)}
\ea
$$

And accordingly,

$$
\ba
\frac
{\E{Y |\red{X_j = a}, \vX_{-j} = \vx_{-j}, T = t}
}
{
\E{Y |\red{X_j = b}, \vX_{-j}=\vx_{-j},T=t}
}
=
\exp{\red{\b_j(a-b)}}
\ea
$$
